column1: Origins: Yahweh may have developed from a Canaanite deity, possibly associated with metallurgy, and the name appears to predate Moses125.
Earliest Attestation: The Mesha Stele (Moabite Stone, c. 850 BCE) is the earliest known non-biblical inscription mentioning Yahweh49.
Biblical Usage: The name YHWH is used throughout the Hebrew Bible, with its meaning traditionally linked to the Hebrew verb "to be" (Exodus 3:14)6789.

Pronunciation: The scholarly consensus is that the original pronunciation was "Yahweh," though the exact vowels are uncertain. Jews eventually ceased to pronounce the name, substituting "Adonai" (Lord) or "Elohim" (God) in reading34789.
Later Developments: The Latinized form "Jehovah" arose in the Middle Ages when Christian scholars combined the consonants YHWH with the vowels of "Adonai"34789.

Sources Used:1 Wikipedia: Yahweh2 World History Encyclopedia: Yahweh Timeline3 Britannica: Yahweh4 Wikipedia: Tetragrammaton5 Big Think: Origin of Yahweh Worship6 World History Encyclopedia: Yahweh7 The Oneness of God: Pronunciation History8 YRM.org: Scholars Attest to Yahweh9 Josh.org: History of the Tetragrammaton
This timeline integrates the development of Yahweh’s name, its earliest attestations, and its evolving religious significance alongside the broader evolution of Israelite and Jewish religion.

column1: Achaemenid Period: Jews benefit from Persian tolerance and administrative structure; Aramaic and Persian customs influence Jewish society.
Greek/Hellenistic Period: Greek language, philosophy, and civic life spread. Some Jews embrace Hellenization (especially elites), while others resist, leading to social and religious tensions.
Resulting Dynamics: The clash between Hellenizing Jews and traditionalists shapes Jewish identity, culminating in the Maccabean revolt and ongoing debates about cultural adaptation.
